text: It is not something we can say about a lot of books written in that period. What van Gennep said was not only new, it was genuinely ground-breaking and indeed foundational. One of the reasons why this is so has exactly to do with the foregrounding of the central, intermediate stage where transition and transformation occur, the liminal aspect that was wholly missing in the approaches offered by the Durkhemians. Third, it is true that van Gennep ‘gallops’ across space and time in his collection of ethnographic data. However, the data itself is sound enough – Mauss has nothing substantial to put his finger on. So what is the problem? Is the data unorganized, as Mauss seems to indicate? The answer is no. The material is extremely convincingly argued and elegantly presented, following through with the tripartite structure, pushing the narrative across the various life-stages of an individual, and for each type of ritual passage drawing into the discussion an otherwise complex ethnographic data set. There were plenty of books written in the period (and since!) that left the reader drowning in data without any clear picture or conceptual frame to hold on to. Van Gennep’s book does not belong there; it is a beacon of light, a fresh breath of analytical clarity and ethnographic rigour in the emerging life sciences. If ever there was ‘grounded theory’, then this was it. 13 And here a spontaneous observation presents itself: if there is one book that resembles Rites of Passage, in terms of structure, procedure, and mode of theorizing, zooming in and out between history, ethnography, etymology and comparative theory, and with a keen perception of another universal tripartite formal structure emerging from the ethnography, then it is another small classic written some years later, and by Marcel Mauss himself: The Gift. It was certainly not a review that did justice to the book, and even the tone of the review leaves the reader with a feeling that Mauss was not sincere. Did the review express Mauss’ own reading, or did he feel forced into defending the Durkheimian position? Or had he in fact been pressed into doing so? L’année Sociologique was Durkheim’s journal; he decided on everything that was to be published there, often down to detail. Mauss’ short and harsh review might simply have been commissioned (co-written?) by Durkheim as pay-back for the earlier critiques launched by van Gennep against Durkheim. Instead of discussing those critiques in merit, actually having to confront them, one simply waits until 13 What Mauss exercises here, is a sheer repetition of a critique that the Durkheimians always launched against members of the British school, including Frazer. Van Gennep was in many ways closer to the British school of anthropology, not least with regard to its empirical foundations, but what Mauss here fails to appreciate is the fact that van Gennep did not simply take over the comparative methodology of the British school. Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 63 van Gennep publishes his next book, and then destroys it. If this is the strategy that Durkheim followed, it can never be proven. Durkheim wrote very few letters to Mauss in that period, and for the simple reason that they now both lived in Paris. From 1907 to 1911, besides his flat on Rue de Cluny, Mauss occupied a room on Rue Saint-Jacques right above Durkheim’s apartment, which he almost certainly used for work (Besnard and Fournier 1998: 349). As we know from the general context of their relationship, Mauss very much lived and worked under Durkheim’s law-like dictates in that period. It is at least safe to say that Mauss was not free to write what he thought about someone who had exposed Durkheim so openly in his earlier writings. And the fact that the relationship between Durkheim and Mauss was becoming extremely strenuous and conflict-ridden in those very years (when World War I broke out, Mauss experienced life in the army as a genuine liberation!) may not be unrelated to what was going on here. Leaving speculation aside, there are however substantial reasons to assume that Mauss eventually took up van Gennep’s work in more than one way, albeit silently so, and without ever paying full credit to his sources of inspiration. Van Gennep’s and Mauss’ interests overlapped in a myriad of ways, and there is one legacy which is worth stressing again here, as it concerns gift-giving, no doubt Mauss’ most influential and important legacy. Van Gennep paid great attention to systems of exchange, and may here have inspired both Mauss and Lévi-Strauss more than has so far been recognized, and more than both Mauss and Lévi-Strauss themselves ever conceded. Chapter 3 14 in The Rites of Passage is almost entirely dedicated to ritualistic exchange of words, gestures, services, goods, slaves, and wives: precisely the aspects of exchange that Mauss systematically took up in his famous essay The Gift, and which Lévi-Strauss used as the platform for his exchange theory. Mauss does not cite or even mention van Gennep’s Rites of Passage in The Gift. And yet, the circulation of gifts is a central aspect of ritualization for van Gennep. Certain ‘gifts’ are obligatorily given, said van Gennep: the circulation of goods and objects serves to create continuous social bonds (1960: 31). Gift giving is the ‘confirmation of a bond’, and ‘to accept a gift is to be bound to the giver’ (ibid.: 29). Van Gennep then discusses, among others, the potlatch as one peculiar example, as he discusses gift-giving practices among warrior groups as a peace- instituting act; van Gennep further notes how incorporation rites are often tied to military, sexual and political rights (ibid.: 35). Van Gennep had been a student of Mauss. But Mauss was also a student of van Gennep. These overlaps and convergences are not surprising: they go back to the joint academic formation of Mauss and van Gennep, and what can be reconstructed as an indeed very similar approach, if not ‘temper’. Van Gennep once
